title: 3-Drawer Open-Shelf Entryway Console
summary: A plywood console with three center-mount drawers above open shelving — a distinct layout from this catalog's other consoles, which pair open shelves with cabinet doors instead.
description:
This console puts three drawers front and center above open shelves rather than combining shelves with hinged doors — a genuinely different storage layout from this catalog's other consoles. Build the main plywood box first, drilling pocket holes facing forward along the underside of the bottom shelf while you're at it, since that's what the front footer will attach to in a later step and it's easier to reach now.

**Attach a 1x4 back support, not the thinner 1x2 shown in some older diagrams** — this plan's own build note upgrades that detail, since the drawer slides need something more substantial than a 1x2 to attach to reliably. Build the front face frame separately and attach it inset and flush to the front of the console.

Build the drawer boxes to fit their actual openings and to the specs of your chosen center-mount slides, adjusting as necessary — this catalog's own shopping-list note recommends **bottom center-mount slides specifically**, which need an additional 1x2 added to the inside front of the console to give the slide hardware something to anchor to; add this piece if you're using that slide type. Install drawers with the front inset 3/4" to allow the drawer face to sit proud, then attach the faces with an 1/8" gap on all sides. The back attaches last with glue and finish nails or staples, and the front gets a final trim pass to finish the piece.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)
Bottom center mount drawer slides, 13 in
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.material: 3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)
3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)
3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)
3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Break the two 3/4" plywood sheets into 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips first. From the 15-3/4" strips cut the top at 46-1/2", two sides at 35-1/4" and the bottom at 45"; from the 14-1/4" strips cut two shelves at 45". From the 1x4s cut the 45" back support, two 15-3/4" side trims, the 48" front trim slide support, six drawer sides at 12" and six drawer fronts and backs at 12-1/2" — three of the four supplied 8 ft boards, with one in reserve. From the 1x2s cut three 45" face-frame members, two side rails at 5-3/4", two end rails at 15-3/4", the 48" top edge trim and four 45" shelf trims; those twelve pieces total about 406" and still leave slack on the five supplied 8 ft 1x2s for the extra inside-front rail the drawer-slide step calls for — pack the short rails onto the offcuts rather than buying a sixth board. From the 1x6 cut three drawer faces at 13-3/4". Cut the 1/4" back panel 46-1/2" by 36" and three drawer bottoms 13-1/2" by 12-1/2".
materials: 3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)

steps.title: Build the main box
steps.body: The two 35-1/4" sides run the full height and the 45" bottom sits between them, which brings the box to 46-1/2" wide overall — exactly the length of the top and the width of the back panel, which is how you know it is right. Set the bottom at the height where you want the open shelf section to start, glue and screw through the sides into its ends, then cap the whole thing with the 46-1/2" top. While the bottom is still open and easy to reach, drill 3/4" pocket holes facing forward along its underside: that is what the front footer screws into later, and there is no getting a drill in there once the console is closed up. Check both diagonals before anything sets.
materials: 3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)

steps.title: Attach the back support
steps.body: Fasten the 45" 1x4 back support between the two sides at the back, level with the top of the drawer bay. It is a 1x4 rather than the 1x2 some older versions of this design show, and that upgrade is deliberate: the centre-mount slides screw into this piece, and a 1x2 does not give them enough to bite into. Attach with pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws. Check it square to both sides, because a slide mounted on a twisted support will bind no matter how well the drawer is built.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame flat on a table before it goes anywhere near the console. Be warned that the cut list's naming does not match its numbers: the three pieces it calls stiles are 45", which is the cabinet's inside width, and the pieces it calls end rails are 15-3/4", which is its depth. So lay all seven pieces out dry on the front of the carcass and settle their positions before you drill a single pocket hole. Join with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, check the assembled frame for square on its own, then glue and clamp it to the front inset and flush. Any twist left in the frame becomes twist in the drawer openings.

steps.title: Trim the top edges
steps.body: Wrap the 46-1/2" top with 1x2 trim cut to fit; the cut list gives one 48" piece, which covers the front with enough left over to cut the miters. Miter the front corners at 45° so no end grain shows from the front, cutting the front piece first and the side returns against it. Glue and pin with 1-1/4" brads into the plywood edge — 3/4" brads will not reach through the trim into the panel, and a plywood edge holds a nail poorly at the best of times, so keep them back from the very end of each piece to avoid splitting.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body: Each drawer box is 12-1/2" wide and 13-1/2" front to back: the 12-1/2" fronts and backs run the full width and the 12" sides sit between them, which is what the 13-1/2" by 12-1/2" drawer bottom proves. Build all three the same way — glue and 1-1/4" brads through the fronts and backs into the ends of the sides — then tack the 1/4" plywood bottom on underneath with 3/4" brads and use it to pull each box square before the glue sets. The 13" centre-mount slides need that full 13-1/2" depth, so do not shorten it to make a box fit. Test each finished box in its own opening before you go any further.

steps.title: Install the drawer slides
steps.body: Bottom centre-mount slides hang under the drawer, so they need a rail at the front as well as the back support already in place: add a 1x2 across the inside of the front opening for the slides to anchor to, set level with the back support so each slide runs dead flat front to back. Mount the cabinet half of each slide first, then the drawer half, following the hardware's own spacing rather than a number from this plan. Set each drawer so its front sits 3/4" back from the face frame — that recess is what lets the drawer face sit proud of the box and land flush with everything around it.
materials: Bottom center mount drawer slides, 13 in

steps.title: Attach drawer faces
steps.body: Cut the three 13-3/4" by 5-1/2" drawer faces from the 1x6 and offer each one up to its drawer with an even 1/8" gap on all four sides. Slip a strip of card or a couple of shims under the bottom edge to hold that gap while you position it, then drive two screws from inside the drawer box out into the back of the face. Pull the drawer right out, add the remaining screws, and slide it back in to check the gaps again before you drill for any hardware.

steps.title: Attach the back and trim the front
steps.body: Fasten the 46-1/2" by 36" back panel on with glue and 1-1/4" brads or staples, nailing into the sides, the top, the bottom and the back support. Tack one corner, confirm the diagonals still match, then nail off at roughly 6" spacing — on a console this wide the back is what stops the box racking. Then fit the remaining front trim: the 48" 1x4 front trim slide support and the two 15-3/4" 1x4 side trims, screwed up into the forward-facing pocket holes you drilled along the bottom in the very first assembly step.

steps.title: Add the shelves
steps.body: Drop the two 45" shelves into the lower section, level and evenly spaced, and fasten them through the sides or with pocket holes from underneath. They are 14-1/4" deep against a 15-3/4" carcass on purpose: the 1x2 shelf trim goes on their front edges and brings them flush with the front of the case. The cut list gives four 45" trim strips for two shelves, which is enough for a strip on the front and the back edge of each — glue and pin them with 1-1/4" brads, keeping the top edges flush with the shelf surface so nothing catches when you slide something off.
materials: 3/4" plywood, full sheets (ripped to 15-3/4" and 14-1/4" strips)

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Iron the edge banding onto every exposed plywood edge before you sand — the top, the side edges and the shelf ends will show raw plies otherwise, which is the one thing that makes a plywood piece read as plywood. Trim the banding flush with a sharp blade, then sand the whole console, easing the top's edges by hand. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly; if you are painting, prime the plywood edges first or they will drink the paint unevenly and stay patchy through two coats.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Entryway console with three drawers above open shelves
